California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
May 10, 1991
carla woodvrorth
Councilmember, Berkeley city Counc i I
218 0 Milvia Street
Berkeley. cA 947 04
Re: Your Request for confirmation of
Telephone Advice
our FiIe No. A-91-2 3 4
Dear Ms. Woodworth:
You are seeking confirmation of telephone advice provided to
you on April- 22, 1991, as an elected member of the Berkeley City
council regarding your duties and responsibilities under the
con f l ict-of- i nterest provisions of the Pol'itical Reform Act (the
rrg.g,1.1 we provi.de you with advice pursuant to Regulation 18329
(copy enclosed).
The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your
telephone conversati-ons with this office on April 22,25 and 29,
1991. and in your fax transmission of April 25, 1991. The latter
communication accurateJ.y represents the advice provided to you on
rrPL LL ".
The commission provides advice onJ.y prospectiveJ-y and does
not provide advice concerning past conduct. You have indicated
that as a result of the telephone advice you abstained from
participation on the natter at a recent city council neeting, but
tnut. 
special neeting could be called if the advice previously
provided to you was changed and your participation permitted.
OUESTION
You are a tenant whose landlord has petitj.oned the locaL rent
board for a rent increase. A hearing is required. The rent board
I co,r..nrn.nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. AII statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations. Section
18500, et seq.- A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
oivision s of tn" California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, suite 8oo . P.o. Box 807 o Sacramenro CA 9i804.!'\807 . (916) 322-5660


File No. A-9■-234
Page 2
is running out of noney, and unless a Ioan is secured it wiII
cease operations for the remainder of the fiscal year. It has
therefore requested a Ioan from the city council. As a
councj,lmember, are you permitted to participate in the decision to
provide funding to the rent board?
CONCLUSTON
You may not participate in the city council decision to
provide funding to the rent board if the decision will have a
foreseeable naterial financial effect on your leasehold property
interest which is not distinguishable fron the effect on the
public generally.
PACTS
The Berkeley Rent Stabilization Program is an independent
branch of city governnent with a separately elected board ('rrent
board"). Anong its functions include holding hearings, upon a
Iandlord's petition, to determine vJhether upward adjustments in
individual rents are warranted. Your landlord has initiated 
such
a petition, and the rent board has preliminarily deternined that
an upward adjustnent of S23O per month, inplemented over a period
of four-to-five years, in your rent will result if the petition is
successful. You occupy your rental property on a month-to-month
tenancy, and currently pay 5278 per month.
The rent board is undergoing fiscal difficulties. 
The city
nanager has estinated that it will have no funds to expend, and
therefore cease operations, as of April 27, L99L. The rent board
has requested a loan of S2oo,Ooo fron the city council to enable
j,t to rneet its operating expenses for the balance of the fiscal
year (untj.l. June 30, 1991).
A hearing on the landlord's petition for a rent adjustment is
scheduled for May 9, L99L, almost two weeks subsequent to the date
on which the rent board's funds are expected to expire. If the
rent board is without sufficient operating funds, it is Iikely
that the hearing on your landlord's petition wi1l, at rninimu:n, be
postponed .
ANALYSTS
A public official 
nay not make, participate in, or use his or
her official 
position to influence a governnental decision in
which the official 
knolrs or has reason to know he or she has a
financial interest. 
(Section 87l,oo. ) A public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decj,sion wil.l have a rnaterial, financial effect,
d ist ingui.shabl,e fron its effect on the public generaLly, on the
official, 
a member of official's 
immediate fanily, or on any real
property in which the official 
has a direct or indirect interest
worth one thousand (51,000) or nore. (Section 87103(b) .)


File No. A-9■-234
Page 3
As an elected member of the city council you are a public
official. 
(section 82048.) If your interest in your property is
worth rnore than one thousand dollars, you nust disqualify yourself
from participating in decisions concerning the proposed
subdivision which will have a reasonably foreseeable :naterial
financial effect on you or your real property interests that is
distinguishable from the effect on the public AeneralIy. (Sectj.on
87103 (b) . )
Foreseeable Fiuancial Effect
i.rhen there is a substantial likelihood that the effect of a
decision wiII occur, it is reasonably foreseeable. l,lhile
certainty is not required, an effect that is merely a possibility
is not reasonably foreseeabLe. (Downev cares v. Downev communitv
Development com. (1987) 196 caI.App.3d 983,' In re Thorner (1975)
I FPPC ops. 198.) The Act, however, does seek to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(Witt v. Morrow
(L977) 70 CaI.App.3d 817, 823.)
on the basis of the fact provided, it appears the city
council's decision whether to loan the rent board operating funds
for the balance of the fiscal year meets the requirement of
reasonable foreseeability. 
One effect of the city council's
decision to provide the loan would be to permit the rent board to
hold the hearing scheduled for May 9, 1991 concerning your
landlord's petition for a rent adjustnent; you have indicated that
if the hearing is held your rent will be increased' The city
council's denial of the requested loan wiII, at minimum, Iikely
postpone the hearing, and thus the rent increase, at Least until
either other funding is secured or until the new fiscal year
commences. In either situation, the foreseeability of the effect
is reasonable.
titaterial Financial Effect
In order for the foreseeable effect of a decision to be
disqualifying, it nust also be material with respect to your real
property interests. 
(Section 87103(b).) The Act defines
I'interest in real property" to include any leasehold interest with
a fair narket value of $1000 or nore. (section 82033.)
In order to deterrnine whether the foreseeable effect of a
decision is naterial as to real property in which an official 
has
a Ieasehold interest, the conmj.ssion has adopted ReguJ.ation
L8702.4 (copy enclosed). Anonq other things, this regulation
provides that the effect of a decision is material as to a
Ieasehold interest in real. property indirectly involved in a
decision if
(d) The decision wil.1 increase the anount of
rent for the leased property by S250 or 5 percent,


File No. A-9■-234
Page 4
$/hichever is greater, during any 12-month period
f ol lov j.ng the decision.
Regulation 18702.4 (d) .
Your participation in a decision on the proposed city council
loan to the rent board would therefore be permitted unless the
value of your leasehol.d property interest was $1,000 or more g-!!X
the decision would result in an increase, at or above a specific
amount, for the property you lease. The impact of the decision on
your rent obligation is a factuaL determination. In providing
advice the Commission does not act as a finder of fact,z and thus
does not evaluate the accuracy of lrhatever facts are presented to
us. You have indicated that the current rent obligatj.on is 5278
per rnonth, and that as a resuLt of the hearing the rent could be
expected to increase approximately 5230 per month, inplemented
over a period of four-to-five years, or approxirnately $50 per
month for each year. To the extent this information is accurate,
it would appear that requirernents of Regulation 18702.4(d) have
been net .
A disqualifying conflict exists, hotrever, only if your
Ieasehold interest is worth 51,ooo or more. (section 87103(b).)
You have informed us that your tenancy is premised on a "month-to-
month'r basis. Regulation 18233 (copy enclosed) provides that
The terns I'interest in real property'r and
I'Ieasehold interest' as used in Government code
section 82033 shaLL not include the interest of a
tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month or Less.
Regulation 18233.
The commission has previously advised that there was no basis
for disqualification under Section 87103(b) pursuant to this
regulation $rhen an official 
occupied a residence on a month-to-
month tenancy. (Vose Advice Letter, I-90-415; Boerer Advice
Letter, I-88-050, copies enclosed. ) However, the Cornmission has
also considered this issue in the context of the city of Berkeley
Rent Stabilization Program and concluded that a month-to-month
tenancy does, in fact, constitute an Iinterest in real propertyt'
for purposes of Sections 87103(b) and 82033 in the city of
Berke ley .
Among the issues considered in In re overstreet (1981) 6 FPPC
ops. 12 (copy enclosed), the Commission was asked whether the rent
board's general counsel could participate in rent board decisions
which would have a naterial financial effect on her interest in
the property she rented on a month-to-month tenancy. The
2 As such, any advice provided is
that all naterial facts have been
presented. (In re oqlesbv, (1975)
applicable only to the extent
accurately and correctJ.y
1 FPPC Ops. 77, copy enclosed. )


File No. A-91-234
Page 5
Comnission concluded that the month-to-nonth tenancy did
constitute an "interest in real property" worth more than $1,000.
Such a conclusion required the finding of a legal entitlement to
occupy the rental unit for a period of several months, so that the
rent paid (S237.50/month) multiplied by the number of rnonths woul,d
exceed the S1,000 figure.
By definition, a nonth-to-nonth tenancy perrnits the landlord
to recover possession at any time upon thirty days notice.
However, under applicable city ordinances ('rMeasure D'r and
subsequent anendnents), a landlord was able to recover possession
only upon a showing that one of a limited number of circumstances
-- e.g., good cause -- existed. As a practical matter, the
Commission concluded in overstreet that under the ordinance most
tenants would be able to occupy the leased property indefinitely
if they continued to pay rent.
Sirnilarly, under city ordinances you are entitled to remain
in occupancy in the leased premises provided you continue to pay
rent. 
Because it is reasonably foreseeable that you are legally
entitled to occupy the rental unit for the requisite period of
time - approximately three and one-half months - to attain the
$1,ooo threshold (three and one-haIf multiplied by S278 nonthly
rental payments) , the prohibitions of section 87103(b) appIy.
Your leasehold interest is thus worth nore than $1,00o.
To the extent the foreseeability and materiality requirenents
of Section 87103(b) and Regulation L8702.4(d) have been rnet, it
would therefore appear that a disqualifying conflict was present
with respect to your participation in the city council's decision
whether to loan operating funds to the rent board.
The t'Public GeDerallyrr Exception
Even if it is your concLusion that a disqualifying conflict
would be present, you are nevertheless stilt 
permitted to
participate in decisions concerning the proposed subdivision if
the effect of the decision on your interests is not
distinguishable fron the effect on the publi.c AeneralIy. (Section
87103 . )
The financial effect of a decision on your leasehold property
interest is distinguishabLe frorn the effect on the public
generally unless the decision's effect on your interest is.
substantially the sane as it is on all nembers of the public or a
significant segTnent of the public. (Regulation l-8703; Jorqensen
ldvice Letter, No. A-90-017; In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In
re owen (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77, copies enclosed. ) The rrpublic
generally" is comprised of the entire jurisdiction of the agency
in question. (In re Leqan, suDra. ) Those facts indicating how,
and uhether, the proposed decision to Loan funds to the rent board
will irnpact all, or I significant segment, of the city's residents
would r;quired assessnent. If a disqualifying conflict exists,


File No. A-9■-234
Page 6
you must determine, based on your circumstances, whether the
"public generally'r exception lrouId appfy to permit your
participation in the loan decisions. s we cannot conclude, on the
basis of the information you have provided, that this exception is
applicable in your situation. {
I hope this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, or wish to supplement your advice request $/ith additional
information, please contact ne at (916) 322-59oL.
S incere Iy ,
Scott
Ha I Iabrin
Genera I
Acti
counsel, Legal Division
SH:」SR:ken
Enclosures
' 
A group that is large in number and heterogeneous in quality
can constitute a significant segment of the public for the
purposes of the pub).ic generally exception. (In re Ferraro (1978)
4 FPPC Ops. 62.) To assj.st you in evaluating the "public
generally" exception, I have enclosed copies of other Cornrnission
advice fetters which have addressed the issue. Enclosed are: EcgE
Advice Letter, No. A-89-243,' Scher Advice Letter, No. A-88-479;
PIvnn Advice Letter, No. I-88-430.
4 In In re overstreet, supra, the Comrnission/s conclusion that
rrtenants in general in Berkeleyrr were considered a significant
segTment of the public generally permitted the participation of the
rent board general counseL in certain rent board decisions,
specif ical),y those concernj.ng the constitutiona l ity or
interpretation of the rent control ordinance that hrould apply to
all tenants in the city. 
This conclusion, however, was fact
specific. 
The effect of litigation 
concerning the rent board on
the general counsel's interests that was distinguishable fron the
effect on tenants in general still 
required disqualj.fication.
While it can be assuned that every tenant is presently not faced
with a Landlord- initiated rent adjustment hearingr before the
board, whether the number of tenants who are subiect to such
proceedings constitutes a significant segment of the public is a
iactual determination for q/hich no inforrnation has been provided.
